prompt: An offline eval reports 92% accuracy, yet users complain heavily about one category of question. What is the most likely explanation, and the right first response?
choices: An aggregate score is hiding a failing subgroup; slice the score by category to expose it
The system has no real problem because 92% is above any reasonable threshold
The model temperature must be lowered to fix all categories at once
Offline evaluation is useless and should be abandoned entirely
explanation: A single 92% average can average over a category that performs terribly, so slicing by category exposes the failing subgroup. Trusting the aggregate ignores the complaints, and temperature changes or abandoning offline eval do not address a hidden per-category failure.

prompt: Which of the following are known biases of an LLM-as-judge that must be mitigated for its scores to be trustworthy? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Position bias (favoring whichever option is presented first)
Verbosity bias (rating longer answers higher regardless of correctness)
Self-preference (favoring outputs from its own model family)
Latency bias (rating faster responses as more accurate)
explanation: Position, verbosity, and self-preference are documented LLM-judge biases that distort scores. 'Latency bias' is not a real judge bias, since the judge scores text content and never sees how fast a response was generated.

prompt: A team optimizes their prompt hard against a single proxy metric until the metric is near-perfect, yet real user satisfaction does not improve. Which principle explains this, and what is the sound practice?
choices: Goodhart's law: when a measure becomes the target it stops being a good measure, so validate against real-world/online signals and multiple metrics
Regression testing: rerun the same metric more often until it converges
Position bias: reorder the dataset so the metric stabilizes
Knowledge cutoff: the metric is stale because the model's training data is old
explanation: Over-optimizing one proxy until it no longer reflects real quality is Goodhart's law, so you validate against real-world signals and several metrics. Rerunning the same metric, reordering data, or blaming the knowledge cutoff do not address the gap between the proxy and actual satisfaction.
